text: My presentation is about robotic lateral lymph node dissection using the data from Shizuoka Cancer Center Hospital. That's my disclosure. According to the Japanese guideline, the indication of the lateral pelvic lymph node dissection is the lower border of the tumor is located to the peritoneum reflection and the tumor depth is more than T three. In this indication, the rate of Lateral lymph node metastasis was reported at twenty percent by Japanese society for cancer of colon and rectum.

Then, this slide shows our strategy for rectal cancer surgeries. Low rectum and tumor depth is more than T-three, we do the bilateral lateral lymph node dissection. On the other hand, if the tumor is upper rectum, or T-two or less, or if patient underwent neoadjuvant chemoradiation therapy, we don't perform prophylactic lateral lymph node dissection. The lymph node dissection area is divided into four parts. Area A is a common iliac node, and area B is an internal iliac node, and area C is an obturator node, and area D is an external iliac node. We usually dissect area A to C.

The video demonstrates a robotic left side lateral pelvic lymph node dissection. The surgeon first mobilizes the autonomic nerves and the right ureter. The procedure includes a common iliac node dissection followed by an obturator lymph node dissection, focusing on the medial aspect.

The surgeon navigates the irregular space between the interneuric arteries and obturator lymph node fatty tissue, identifying the inferior vesicular artery and reaching the pelvic floor. The levator ani muscle is visible, along with the lateral aspect of the obturator lymph node dissection. The external iliac node and vessels are exposed near the pubic bone. The distal end of the obturator arteries and nerves are dissected along the main trunk of the internal iliac vein. The root of the obturator arteries and the obturator vein flowing to the internal iliac vein are visible. Dissection continues along the internal pudendal artery, revealing the coccyx muscle covering its distal end.

The surgeon mobilizes the distal end of the inferior vesicle vessels, revealing the white pelvic splanchnic nerve. The dissection plane reaches the pelvic floor, exposing the thick fascia covering the bladder and vesicular arteries or veins. The obturator lymph node dissection is completed, and the internal iliac node dissection is visible between the autonomic nerves, including the hypogastric nerve and pelvic plexus. The second and third pelvic splanchnic nerves are also visible, marking the distal part of the internal iliac node dissection.

The video shows a complex case of lateral lymph node dissection where the metastatic lymph node invades the main trunk of the internal iliac vein. The surgeon first separates the internal iliac artery and then the distal vein from the pelvic wall. Finally, the main trunk of the internal iliac vein is separated or ligated, with visible small and large vessels flowing into it. The sacral plexus is also visible during the procedure.

And this is our consecutive two hundred ten robotic lateral lymph node dissection in Cesar Kansas Center Hospital. The tumor distance from the inner verge was only four point five centimeters and the tumor stage were almost three or two and four. and more than two-thirds of the patients were underwent bilateral lateral lymph node dissection. Even in this tumor allocation and tumor stage, only ten percent of patients were needed for neoadjuvant chemoradiation therapy. For comparison of oncological outcome, the patient was stratified into the seventy-eight robotic and open lateral lymph node dissection groups. And this is those data. The local recurrence pre-survivor in robotic group was significantly better than open lateral lymph node dissection groups. In my conclusion, the robotic surgery is a good indication for lateral lymph node dissection. Thank you very much for your attention.
